entry.title: Accelerate the Budget Approval Process with SAP HANA Cloud vector engine
entry.content:
About Sierra’s OhZone SBF:

Sierra has introduced a tailored BTP application known as SBF – Smart Budgeting and Forecasting. The solution  is recently SAP ICC certified too. Have a look at it in the Certified Solutions Directory. The innovative tool streamlines the entire budgeting process, from the initial request through to final approval. With the ability to create dynamic form layouts and manage workflows, SBF enhances budgeting efficiency. Integrated with SAC – Planning, it supports detailed planning and facilitates team collaboration. Users can also export data to Excel and attach it as supporting documentation to their budget requests.

          Pic 1: Process Workflow

  • Multiple budget form requests and workflows can be handled with the process in an effective and seamless way.
  • Tailored for Budgeting Team, this solution transforms budgeting and forecasting processes, offering a seamless, AI-driven approach for managing both capital and operational budgets across departments.
  • It streamlines every phase, from initiation to final approval, enabling predictive insights for smarter decision-making.
  • This all-in-one platform represents the future of efficient, strategic financial planning.

How has Gen AI and SAP HANA Cloud Vector enhanced OhZone SBF?

Case 1: Gen AI

The form layouts for different organizations and even for different user groups within the same organization vary widely. We have a low-code no-code, drag and drop custom form builder where a super user can create the forms and publish them for users.

Though we have this low-code no-code, dynamic form builder, still the super user needs to create multiple budget request form layouts depending on different departments or different user groups.

Gen AI – Solution Enhancement

Sierra has used the Gen AI API from within the SBF application to create the first draft of the form by just entering a prompt. We have used the GPT 4.0 model in the SAP AI Core.

Form Generation:

  • Leverage the  Large Language Model (LLM) for form generation. The context is initialized with form component building blocks, and based on the user's prompt, the form is generated using  LLM.
  • The process starts with setting up the integration between the systems to ensure smooth communication and data exchange.
  • User requirements and templates are provided as input for Gen AI to generate the form structure. The response JSON from LLM is then processed and rendered as a form for the end user

 

Watch a demo here on how Sierra has leveraged the SAP generative AI hub to generate budget request forms in its SBF application.

 

Demo Video Link: https://youtu.be/Pv9iZV5zDMY  

 

Pic 2: Dynamic Form Builder Prompt

 

 

Pic 3: Dynamic Form Builder Prompt Result

 

Value Proposition for customers

By prompting, SAP Generative AI Hub helps users simply and quickly create custom, dynamic budget request form layouts. They experience a significantly improved and automated approach to designing multiple form layouts. Our solution streamlines the process, enabling faster and more efficient creation of diverse forms with ease. This enhancement not only saves time but also ensures accuracy and consistency across all your form layouts

Case 2: SAP HANA Cloud Vector

While initiating and filling the budget request form, the requestors are used to attaching large PDFs, excel files, word documents as a substantiating documents like Policies , Union Collective bargaining agreements , Government Orders or policies, Market data etc. The Approver has to read all this document which is a time-consuming process and makes it difficult to take a decision in a short time .

 

SAP HANA Cloud vector – Solution Enhancement

Summarization of long documents and querying using Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG)with the  SAP HANA Cloud Vector Engine enables quicker understanding of the attachments to budget request forms and allows taking informed decisions while approving the budget requests.​ This and eases the process of Budget approvals significantly. Initially, it took 30 minutes to create the form for reading, but now the process takes only 5 minutes, resulting in a 25-minute time savings.

 

    SAP HANA Cloud Vector Engine:

  • Different unstructured documents like Budget Manuals are converted to Vector embeddings using an embedding model.
  • We have used text-embedding-ada-002 from Azure Open AI Foundational models in the SAP Gen AI Hub to convert the text into embeddings.
  • Vector embeddings are stored in the SAP HANA Cloud, SAP HANA database

 

Demo Video Link: https://youtu.be/8Qk5yMcyLeE

Pic 4: HANA Cloud Vector

Value Proposition for Customers

Our solution dramatically accelerates the budget approval process by expediting the review of attached documents. It allows for simultaneous querying and analysis of multiple documents using natural language. This remarkably streamlines the review workflow. The gained efficiency not only speeds up the approval timeline but also ensures a thorough and organized examination of all relevant materials, enhancing overall .

In a short period of time, more approvals can be completed as the approver can process documents 70% faster, significantly reducing the overall time required.

 

 

High-Level Process Flow:

  • The architecture connects different SAP services and systems (S/4HANA, HANA Cloud, Analytics Cloud) in a secure and AI-augmented environment, ensuring seamless data flow and integration.
  • Application Clients, access services through the Launchpad Service, authenticated by the Identity Authentication Service.
  • Application data processing and storage aspects, that interact with SAP HANA Cloud include SAP BTP Apps, which run on Node.js and HDI Container.
  • Secure communication between the SAP BTP and SAP S/4HANA is facilitated by the Cloud Connector, using HTTP OData over a secure tunnel.
  • Data from SAP S/4HANA can be imported into SAP Analytics Cloud to provide analytics and reporting functionalities. It has the integrated tool Just Ask, which could involve natural language queries, indicating AI-driven insights.

 

Pic 5: SBF Architecture

 

For Additional Information:

  • SBF ICC Certificate
  • SBF SAP Store list

entry.title: Procedure For End-To-End Encryption And Signing Of File Through SFTP Via Integration Suite PART-2
entry.content:
I have a requirement where I must process a payment file generated in SAP through integration suite by encrypting and signing the file, sending the file to the bank server. We are sending and receiving the payment file through SFTP, to connect to the bank server, we have provided them with a PUBLIC SSH KEY. We were given the public key of the receiver, folder path to place the file, server address and credentials of the same to login and we must process the file generated in SAP to the receiver by encrypting and signing the file. We should encrypt the data using receiver’s public key and sign the same using our private key. I have discussed the keys part in my first blog in this blog i would like to share the details regarding integration flow part, if any one have missed any details regarding the first part I am sharing the link here for your access to the first blog,

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-members/procedure-for-end-to-end-encryption-and-signing-of-file-through-sftp-via/ba-p/13873725

Once you have successfully uploaded the keys in PGP keys and after testing the connectivity of source and target destinations, we now have configured the sender and receiver configuration, I have developed my integration flow, please find below image,

 

I had captured the file which came from SAP in the content modifier and now I have the necessary data to be encrypted and signed, let us now see the configuration of PGPEncryptor, In PGPEncryptor please go to processing and there you can find the first option where is says signatures, as shown below,

 

 

  • I must encrypt the data and sign the same, so I am including the signatures.
  • You can select the content encryption algorithm, secret key length and compression algorithm according to your requirement, I had changed the compression algorithm to uncompressed as I don’t want to compress the data.
  • There is an option of armored, if you have enabled/ticked/checked the box then the resulting PGP message will be encoded with a specific header in base64.
  • You can check the integrity protected data packet if you want to create a symmetrically encrypted and integrity protected data packet.
  • Now you can find the option of encryption key user IDs from public keyring where you must add public key of the receiver which we have earlier uploaded in PGP Keys, also find the signer key user IDs where we must add our private/secret key which we have generated using GPA tool earlier and uploaded to the PGP keys, please find the image below,

 

 

Now that you have done with configuration of encryption and signing, let me also show you the configuration for decryption as shown below,

 

 

  • In PGPDecryptor there is no configuration for the decryption unless you want to verify the signature, where you receive the data which is encrypted and signed.
  • As you are receiver in the case of decryptor you require the public key of the sender to verify the signature of the sender.

I would like to share my point that this requirement is configured for host to host configuration with bank and customer.

So, I conclude the whole requirement where we have seen generating SSH KEYS using PUTTY KEY GENERATOR, generating KEY PAIR using GPA TOOL, processing the payment file from SAP to BANK server by encrypting and signing the file.

Please provide me your valuable feedback and if you any queries feel free to share.

entry.title: Configuring OAuth 2.0 between SAP C4C and SAP BTP
entry.content:
You need to create HTTP destinations in the SAP BTP cockpit. These destinations provide data communication via HTTP protocol.

You can use the SAML Bearer assertion flow for consuming OAuth-protected resources. Users are authenticated by using SAML against the configured trusted identity providers. The SAML assertion is then used to request an access token from an OAuth authorization server. This access token must be added as an Authorization header with value Bearer <access token> in all HTTP requests to the OAuth-protected resources.

WORKFLOW

  1. Configure OAuth Identity Provider in SAP Cloud for Customer.
  2. Configure the OAuth Client for OData Access to SAP Cloud for Customer OData APIs.
  3. Create and Configure the HTTP Destination.

1. Configure OAuth Identity Provider in SAP Cloud for Customer:

  1. Log on to the SAP BTP cockpit, and configure the settings as below,
    1. Navigate to your extension subaccount in the Cloud Foundry environment.
    2. Choose Connectivity ->Destination Trust.
    3. Choose Export to get the active trust certificate for this subaccount and save it on your local file system.                                                              
    4. Open the certificate in a text editor, copy the content between -----BEGIN CERTIFICATE----- and -----END CERTIFICATE-----, and save it in a file with the following format <subaccount>_signing.cer, where <subaccount> is the Subaccount Name from the Subaccount Information of your subaccount.
  2. Log on to your SAP Cloud for Customer system as an administrator. Go to ADMINISTRATOR ->Common Tasks.
  3. Choose Configure OAuth 2.0 Identity Provider ->New OAuth2.0 Provider, and configure the settings as below,                                                                                                                                            
    1. In the Issuing Entity Name field, enter cfapps.<region_host>/<subaccountID>. You go to the SAP BTP cockpit, navigate to the subaccount, go to Overview and,
      • For the <region_host>, copy the API endpoint from the Cloud Foundry section, and remove the https://api.cf..
      • For the <subaccountID>, copy the ID from the Subaccount Details section.
      • For example: cfapps.eu10.hana.ondemand.com/12345678-1234-1234-1234-1234578.
    2. From the Primary Signing Certificate field, browse the <subaccount>_signing.cer file that you saved.
    3. Select the E-Mail Address checkbox.
    4. Choose Submit.

Here is an example for OAuth 2.0 Identity Provider:                                 

2. Configure the OAuth Client for OData Access to SAP Cloud for Customer OData APIs:

  1. Log on to your SAP Cloud for Customer system as an administrator.
  2. Go to ADMINISTRATOR ->OAUTH2.0 CLIENT REGISTRATION.
  3. Choose New,                                                        
  4. Specify a client secret, description, and token lifetime (in seconds). For example, 3600 seconds.
  5. In the Issuer Name field, use the dropdown list to specify the identity provider that you created.
  6. Copy the entry in the Client ID field. You will need it later when creating the HTTP destination with OAuth authentication required for the connectivity to the SAP Cloud for Customer OData APIs.                                     
  7. In the Scope list, select the UIWC:CC_HOME scope.
  8. Choose Save and Close.                                            

3. Create and Configure the HTTP Destination:

  1. Log on to your SAP BTP cockpit, navigate to your subaccount.
  2. Choose Connectivity ->Destinations in the navigation panel.
  3. Create an HTTP destination.
  4. To enable principal propagation, create an OAuth2SAMLBearerAssertion HTTP destination,                                     
    1. Configure the basic settings:                                                                     
      ParameterValue
      NameEnter a meaningful name.
      TypeHTTP
      Description(Optional) Enter a meaningful description.
      URLhttps://<my_SAP_Cloud_for_Customer_system_name>.crm.ondemand.com/sap/c4c/odata/v1/c4codataapi
      Proxy TypeInternet
      AuthenticationOAuth2SAMLBearerAssertion
      AudienceGo to the SAP Cloud for Customer administration view, then navigate to Configure Single Sign-On under General Settings and copy the value from the Local Service Provider field.
      Client KeyClient ID
      Paste the entry you have copied from the Client ID field when configuring the OAuth client.
      Token Service URLhttps://<my_SAP_Cloud_for_Customer_system_name>.crm.ondemand.com/sap/bc/sec/oauth2/token
      Token Service UserClient ID
      Paste the entry you have copied from the Client ID field when configuring the OAuth client.
      Token Service PasswordClient secret
      Paste the entry you have copied from the Client Secret field when configuring the OAuth client.
    2. Configure the required additional property. To do so, in the Additional Properties panel, choose New Property, and enter the following property:                                                                                   
      ParameterValue
      scopeScope ID entries separated by space.
      Paste the entry you have copied from the Client ID field when configuring the OAuth client.
      Example: UIWC:CC_HOME
    3. Select the Use default JDK truststore checkbox.
    4. Save your entries.

Here is an example for a destination definition and additional parameters:

In this way, we can configure the OAuth 2.0 between SAP Cloud for Customer and SAP Business Technology Platform.

Thanks,
Arun

entry.title: Generic Groovy script to log multiple custom headers in BTP Cloud integration iFlow runtime
entry.content:
SAP Cloud integration Apache camel framework provides generic method addCustomHeaderProperty to log custom header and there are lot of documentation around the same on how to log.

In this blog, we can see how we can use generic script to log any number of custom headers and use this method from integration flow based on specific interface needs.

 

Create generic script collection with below groovy script code:

logCustomHeader.groovy

Code:

def Message processData(Message message) {
    def count = 0;
    def list = message.getProperties().keySet();
    for( l in list ) {
        if (l.trim().toLowerCase().startsWith("loggingkey")) {
            count = count +1;
        }
    }
    if(count>0){
        for (i=1;i<=count;i++){
            def messageLog = messageLogFactory.getMessageLog(message);
            if(messageLog != null){         messageLog.addCustomHeaderProperty(message.getProperty("LoggingKey"+i),message.getProperty("LoggingValue"+i));
            }
        }
    }
    return message;
}

From integration flow, we can populate these values as exchange property (either from Content modifier or from code):

 

In this example, we can simply see that we used constants, but we could use xpath or other options in content modifier.

After properties are populated in some ways, we could call this global references groovy script:

 

In Cloud integration runtime monitoring, we can see these headers:

To conclude, this blog provides quick tip or generic common function which can be use to log any number of custom headers to be displayed on Cloud integration Runtime of iFlow.

entry.title: Enhance Task Approvals with the Power of AI
entry.content:
In today’s fast-paced corporate environment, delays in task approvals can significantly hamper productivity, create bottlenecks, and escalate costs. When approval processes are slow, employees often face frustration, morale dips, and businesses run the risk of missing critical opportunities or failing to meet regulatory compliance deadlines. Particularly in industries that thrive on innovation, these delays can obstruct competitiveness and diminish the organization’s ability to respond swiftly to market changes.

To address these challenges, Task Center is launching a revolutionary AI-powered feature called Intelligent Recommendations. This functionality leverages artificial intelligence to streamline and accelerate task approval processes by analyzing key attributes relevant to specific business cases and generating predictive models for task approvals.

The initial version of Intelligent Recommendations focuses on tasks created using SAP Build Process Automation (SBPA)—a cloud-based workflow management solution—with plans to expand compatibility with other providers in future updates.

Prerequisites:

To use the Intelligent Recommendations feature, the SBPA service must be instantiated within the tenant, as the AI engine resides there. This initial implementation only supports process automation through SBPA, utilizing data that can be accessed from the SBPA engine.

User Perspectives on Intelligent Recommendations

Business User:

From the business user’s perspective, Intelligent Recommendations operates on a per-task-type level. A critical element of this functionality is the Confidence Level, which determines the AI’s confidence in automatically approving tasks based on the specified criteria.

Task Type Selection:
Begin by selecting the desired task type and clicking the "Go" button. This action will display the confidence level field, where approval conditions can be defined.    

Setting Confidence Levels:
Users can specify a confidence level for automatic task approvals. If the confidence level falls below 70%, the task will not qualify for automatic approval. Available options for defining the confidence level include: Less than,Equal to, Between and Greater than. 

                     

Note:
Clicking the "Go" button is essential when setting up the confidence level, as this ensures the changes are applied correctly and helps optimize system performance. 

 

Sorting by Confidence Levels:

Once the confidence level is set, it becomes a sortable column within the task table. This enables users to prioritize tasks based on their confidence level, facilitating a more organized and efficient approval process

Administrator View: Managing Intelligent Recommendations

Administration Cockpit:

Administrators have access to the Intelligent Recommendations tab within the Task Center’s administration cockpit. This section provides a comprehensive overview of all models, allowing administrators to create, manage, and adjust AI-powered task approval models as business requirements evolve.

Steps to Create a Model:

  1. Accessing the Model Creation Feature:
    To create a new model, Go to Administration cockpit  and Select Intelligent Reccomendations tab. click on the "Create Model" button.                                   

    Please note that only authorized administrators or key users can perform this action, so ensure the appropriate user roles are in place.

  2. Filling in the Model Details:
    Upon clicking "Create Model," a pop-up window will appear with the following fields: 
      • Task Provider: At present, only SAP Build Process Automation (SBPA) is available as a task provider.
      • Task Type: Select the appropriate task type from the available dropdownlist.
      • Model Name: Provide a unique name for the model, which will be used to identify it in the list of models.
      • Description: Enter a description that reflects the model's purpose from administrative point of view.                                                      
  3. Selecting Task Type Attributes:
    Administrators can choose from a list of available task attributes:
    • Categorical Attributes: These are static attributes that remain consistent ver time.
    • Numerical Attributes: Dynamic attributes that may change, reflecting variations in the business environment.
  4. Completing the Model Creation:
    After configuring the model, click "Create". The newly created model will appear in the list with its initial status as either Active or In Training.                    
  5. Status and Activation:
    Once the AI engine has analyzed all relevant tasks, the model’s status will update to Ready for Activation. A Benchmark value will be displayed, indicating the model’s reliability and effectiveness. This benchmark is calculated based on several factors, and the data science team can provide more details on its derivation.
  6. Model Management:
    • Activation: Once ready, the model can be activated and put into use. If needed, models can also be deactivated to accommodate shifting business needs.
    • Retraining the Model: If the benchmark is unsatisfactory, administrators can retain the model by adjusting the selected attributes. This flexibility ensures that the model remains relevant as business requirements evolve.
  7. Conclusion:

    The Intelligent Recommendations feature within Task Center introduces an AI-driven approach to task approvals, providing businesses with the ability to significantly reduce bottlenecks, enhance operational efficiency, and maintain compliance with critical deadlines. By allowing both business users and administrators to define confidence levels, manage models, and retrain them as necessary, this functionality empowers organizations to stay agile and competitive in an increasingly fast-paced market.

    As more providers are integrated into future releases, the scope of Intelligent Recommendations will continue to grow, offering even more opportunities for businesses to harness the power of AI in their workflows.

     

    Product Links:

    https://help.sap.com/docs/task-center/sap-task-center/working-with-intelligent-recommendations

    https://statics.teams.cdn.office.net/evergreen-assets/safelinks/1/atp-safelinks.html

    Enjoy!!

entry.title: LEVEREDGE 2024 Highlights: Discovering New Strategies for Digital Transformation
entry.content:
Organizations face a multitude of challenges today, from adapting to rapid technological advancements to navigating complex regulatory environments. LEVEREDGE 2024 was created to address these pressing issues and to provide a platform where industry leaders come together to explore the latest strategies, technologies, and solutions shaping the future of business.

Over three days, this premier event gathered 400+ guests and thought leaders from 10+ countries to share insights, experiences, and success stories. Attendees had the opportunity to engage in diverse sessions, including keynote presentations, panel discussions, and interactive workshops, covering vital topics such as Business AI, Digital Supply Chain, Intelligent Spend & Procurement, Data Migration, S/4HANA, Product Lifecycle Management, and much more.

Key Highlights of Day 1

The event kicked off with a Design Thinking Networking Breakfast, setting the tone for meaningful connections among participants. This engaging atmosphere fostered discussions that sparked new ideas and collaborative solutions.

The day continued with Design Thinking Workshops led by the LeverX AppHaus Team and Andreas Hauser, Senior Vice President of the SAP AppHaus Network, where participants explored AI-driven solutions.

Further collaborative discussions emphasized the potential of AI and fostered a spirit of collaboration, encouraging attendees to address the challenges businesses face on their digital transformation journeys.

Key Highlights of Day 2

Day 2 of LEVEREDGE 2024 was all about deepening our understanding of the latest advancements in digital transformation, artificial intelligence, and innovative business practices.

Spanning three dynamic stages — the Blue Stage, Green Stage, and the Yellow Stage (SAP PLM Forum) — the agenda featured keynotes from industry leaders, expert panels, and interactive discussions, each offering valuable perspectives on the evolving role of technology in today’s business landscape. From AI-powered solutions to groundbreaking PLM innovations, the day was packed with insightful conversations, practical takeaways, and plenty of opportunities to network with peers and thought leaders.

 

🔵 Blue Stage highlights

Opening remarks: Motivational speeches from Dr. Victor Lozinski, Co-Founder and Chairman of the Board at LeverX, and Daniella Levine Cava, Mayor of Miami-Dade County.

  • Maximizing business efficiency: How leveraging SAP technology can unlock new levels of operational efficiency and enhance customer experiences.
  • AI in research & development: The role of AI in accelerating research and development processes, particularly in the pharmaceutical sector, driving faster innovation.
  • AI & digitalization for business sustainability: An exploration of how AI and digitalization are transforming business models, driving sustainability, and supporting growth in various industries.
  • Data modernization: The critical importance of modernizing data strategies to maintain a competitive edge in today’s rapidly evolving market.
  • Innovative service solutions: Multi-platform service applications that deliver enhanced operational capabilities and customer value were showcased.
  • Leadership & technology: How effective leadership can help organizations navigate technological advancements and leverage them for business growth.

🟢 Green Stage highlights

  • AI-powered Clean Core extensions: How SAP Business Technology Platform enhances key extensions with AI to drive innovation and operational efficiency.
  • Cybersecurity and AI: An exploration of the collaborative role of AI and Governance, Risk, and Compliance (GRC) solutions in improving SAP cybersecurity strategies.
  • Generative AI in enterprise operations: The benefits of integrating generative AI solutions from cloud platforms like AWS to transform enterprise operations.
  • ERP transformation success: A case study on the successful implementation of S/4HANA to streamline ERP transformation in the manufacturing sector.
  • Warehousing innovations: Emerging trends and innovations in SAP warehousing solutions that optimize supply chain processes.
  • Enhanced employee experiences: The discussion on how mobile technology, AI, and user-centered design to improve employee engagement and experiences.
  • HR transformation with SAP SuccessFactors: The journey of implementing SAP SuccessFactors to drive operational excellence in human resource management.
  • Digital transformation in the food industry: A real-world example of digital transformation within the food and beverage industry, focusing on improving operational efficiency and customer experience.

🟡Yellow Stage highlights

  • SAP PLM vision & strategy: An exploration of the latest developments and future strategies for SAP Product Lifecycle Management (PLM), focusing on innovation and business growth.
  • Global PLM implementation success: A real-world case study demonstrating the successful implementation of a global PLM system in discrete industries, highlighting its transformative effects on product lifecycle management.
  • Panel on PLM trends and challenges: A discussion of key trends in digital twins, product design, and the critical role of integrating PLM into enterprise resource planning (ERP) systems to drive business efficiency.

Breakout sessions:

  • Specification management & formulation: A deep dive into process PLM, focusing on the complexities of managing product specifications and formulation processes.
  • Integrating digital twins in PLM and ERP: An exploration of how digital twins are becoming an essential component of product design and how they integrate with SAP's PLM and ERP solutions.

 

As Day 2 of LEVEREDGE 2024 came to a close, a clear theme emerged: the critical role that AI, digital transformation, and innovative leadership play in shaping the future of business. As industries continue to innovate, the integration of AI, data-driven solutions, and leadership will remain key drivers of sustainable growth and transformation.

Key Highlights of Day 3

Day 3 of LEVEREDGE 2024 was all about embracing the transformative power of AI, digital transformation, and leadership innovation. As the final day of the event unfolded, two primary stages — the Blue Stage and the Yellow Stage (SAP PLM Forum) — hosted a range of insightful sessions. 

🔵 Blue Stage highlights

  • AI for business efficiency: Introduction to the practical integration of AI into business operations, focusing on enhancing efficiency and scalability.
  • AI in logistics: Insights on how AI is transforming logistics, improving delivery precision, and optimizing supply chain management.
  • Leadership and innovation: A session exploring the intersection of leadership and technology, offering lessons from leadership journeys and innovative business practices.
  • Digital transformation case studies: Real-world examples of how businesses are leveraging AI, mobile technology, and SAP solutions to drive digital transformation and operational excellence.
  • AWS and SAP BTP for innovation: Discussion on how AWS and SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) are enabling organizations to innovate and streamline processes for better business outcomes.
  • Mobile work order management: A demonstration of the technology behind mobile work order management, showcasing its ability to simplify workflows and enhance operational efficiency.

AI-powered solutions in action: Use cases illustrating how AI-powered solutions are driving industry transformation, with examples of businesses adopting AI to enhance their operations.

  • Digital transformation in transportation: Insights into how companies are using SAP S/4HANA and Transportation Management to achieve operational efficiencies in the transportation sector.
  • Maximizing SAP investments: An exploration of the strategic benefits of engaging with user groups to fully unlock the potential of SAP investments.
  • Managing vendor and supply chain risks: Best practices for navigating risks in vendor and supply chain management, with a focus on procurement and mitigation strategies.

🟡 Yellow Stage (SAP PLM Forum) highlights

  • Sustainable innovation: An exploration of how PLM drives innovation while ensuring sustainability and compliance.
  • AI in product development: How AI improves the efficiency of new product development processes.
  • PLM success stories: Real-world examples of PLM implementations, showcasing business transformation.

PLM security: Best practices for securing PLM applications and protecting data.

  • Product data governance: Managing product data and engineering changes effectively.
  • Industry-specific roadmaps: Future advancements in PLM for discrete and process industries.

As Day 3 concluded, a key takeaway was the undeniable impact of AI and digital transformation on industries across the board. From revolutionizing logistics and supply chains to driving sustainable innovation through PLM solutions, the sessions underscored how technology is reshaping modern business practices. The importance of leadership, collaboration, and continuous learning was evident, as attendees left with fresh perspectives and strategies to implement in their own organizations. LEVEREDGE 2024 concluded with a clear message: innovation, driven by AI and digital tools, is the future of business success.

The Bottom Line

LEVEREDGE 2024 showcased the future of business, emphasizing the pivotal role of AI, digital transformation, and leadership in driving innovation. Across three days of insightful sessions, industry leaders and experts shared their visions and practical strategies for navigating the evolving business landscape. From AI-powered logistics to sustainable PLM solutions, the event highlighted how technology is reshaping industries. As attendees leave equipped with fresh perspectives, the key takeaway is clear: embracing innovation and leveraging digital tools are essential for achieving lasting business success.

entry.title: HANA indexserver crashing during backups on RHEL9
entry.content:
Hello,

I have a HANA DB on version 2.00.075.00 with Red Hat Linux 9.4. I am experiencing an issue on my HANA DB where during some backups, performance of the DB is severely degraded with numerous I/O errors showing on the OS. It will eventually keep throwing I/O errors until the HANA directory is unmounted from the system, thus resulting in HANA crashing. This has happened at least three times, and in the three crash dumps I have, they all list this call stack:

Regs: rbx=0x0, rbp=0x7e31f3fc4dd0, r12=0x7f4b5dc472f0, r13=0x0, r14=0x7f4b8e1c3100, r15=0x7f4d8aad3490
Source: DataAccess/PageAccess/impl/PageIOImpl.cpp:200
Module: /usr/sap/HEP/HDB00/exe/libhdbdataaccess.so
-----------------------------------------
10: FileAccess::SingleFileIOControlBlock::reportError(char const*, unsigned long, char const*, int) + 0xbf
Symbol: _ZN10FileAccess24SingleFileIOControlBlock11reportErrorEPKcmS2_i
SFrame: IP: 0x00007f4d8a6d5f4f (0x00007f4d8a6d5e90+0xbf) FP: 0x00007f4b5dc47380 SP: 0x00007f4b5dc472c0 RP: 0x00007f4d8a677fa9
Regs: rbx=0x101, rbp=0x7e31f3fc4e10, r12=0x7f4b5dc472f0, r13=0x0, r14=0x7f4b8e1c3100, r15=0x7f4d8aad3490
Source: Basis/IO/FileAccess/impl/SingleFileCallback.cpp:161
Module: /usr/sap/HEP/HDB00/exe/libhdbbasis.so

 

 

This part in the crash dump led me to finding this note: 3318049 - Indexserver Crashes at __pthread_kill_implementation When Running on RHEL 9.x (https://me.sap.com/notes/3318049/). The symptoms described in this note are exactly what our systems are experiencing, however it says the solution is to upgrade to Revisions >= 070.00 (SPS07) when we are already on 2.00.075. Support continues to say that it is an issue with our underlying hardware or OS, however seeing that our crash dump is almost identical to the one in the note, I am beginning to think otherwise. Our hardware and OS vendors have not been able to find any discrepancies in our systems. This issue has been randomly occurring for over 2 months on prod DBs causing our backup process to be shaky and this note is the closest thing I have seen to addressing the problem.

Has anyone else that has ran HANA on RHEL9 ever experienced this issue or similar?

entry.title: Tailored IT strategy planning: The key to successfully harmonized business and IT goals
entry.content:
In recent conversations with various IT leaders on their projects and challenges, answers on the purpose of further IT implementations are, as expected, considerably multilayered. They range from “simple” IT cost-saving goals (TCO reduction) and security aspects (cybersecurity) to the adaptation, or even the creation of entirely new business models.  

The following diagram shows a snapshot of the challenges that companies routinely consider, divided across business-driven and underlying IT topics:

Additional concerns also emerged from these discussions, including complexity, silos, and a lack of coordinated plans across the different areas. They revealed that stakeholders underestimated the interdependence and need for coherent planning. Subprojects often start before realizing the importance in having a comprehensive plan, and as a result, the projects become inconsistent.

When working on a single project, one goal may be attainable throughout the project. However, when the project is part of a larger program or initiative, its shortcomings become transparent and overall target achievement is far removed. In addition to project delays, the initially saved effort – whether in time or money – leads to frustration among stakeholders who fulfilled their goals from their project perspective.

While a siloed focus on local interests is challenging, so is selecting one or more partners for the transformation. This is where the shortcomings of a missing coherent planning basis become clear. Decoupled subproject requests for anything (RFXs) result in individual quotations that fall short of building an overarching, comprehensive transformation plan.

Unraveling project challenges

Thinking beyond ready-made solutions and services and standardized procedures and delivery objects can be challenging when addressing complex issues. For that reason, a business transformation advisor can be beneficial. The advisor enables SAP customers to determine whether tailored services are necessary and which methods and tools are needed early in the full-scope planning process.

Like a tailor creating a custom suit, a business transformation advisor defines a tailored consulting service by using the following process: 

  1. Collect mandate and define scope: A clear definition of the mandate and scope should be approved and supported by management during the project planning phase. This involves all elements of the business strategy, including the mission, target markets, current and future product and service offerings, and sales and people strategies. Topics, such as AI and sustainability, must be appropriately considered – after all, plans are being made for the next 5 to 10 years. This helps ensure a holistic IT strategy is defined and aligned with all company goals.
  2. Recognize and understand the strategic direction: The intent and path forward must be carefully considered. Key questions include who represents the company strategy and its drivers in this endeavor, what decision criteria can be derived from the strategic direction of the business, and how the mandate’s objectives match the plan’s objectives.
  3. Define decision criteria: All subsequent steps and deliverables must align with the company's goals. Which deliverables will be required to make strategic decisions? How does the decision-making process for products and projects work? Who is involved, and what are their respective priorities and requirements?
  4. Define the procedure model: Picking suitable methods and tools for developing defined delivery objects is critical. Different criteria play a role in the selection, such as who can adequately represent the business and provide information about current needs and future projects, where knowledge can be found, and which tools are needed to analyze process performance and systems. In addition, the effectiveness of the chosen project methodology must be assessed in light of business circumstances.
  5. Determine success factors: Implementing measures to achieve goals and ensure compliance becomes increasingly difficult throughout the project when specific factors are unidentified, undefined, or poorly communicated. Critical factors include active management sponsorship, sufficient time and budget, potential resistance, and appropriate countermeasures. Above all, involving the right and most important partners – such as the primary software supplier – is essential, with their accountability being key.

The outcome of this process – when clearly aligned and tailored to business goals – forms a stable digital IT foundation for the company’s strategy. This foundation, in turn, is the basis for implementing the corporate strategy, by which a customer’s CIOs, chief digital officers (CDOs), and their teams are ultimately measured.

The next step is selecting appropriate partners for the implementation. The RFx can only be carried out successfully if a clear and solid basis has been developed. Specifically, this refers to the necessary deliverables and general conditions mentioned earlier to evaluate and compare offers meaningfully.

Companies often do not clearly define the essential elements of a transformation in their RFPs. Typical examples include an unclear process scope, undefined migration approach or exclusions, and lacking integration of third-party products and add-ons. As a result, quotations cannot be adequately compared, because they differ in content, effort estimates and price and are unaligned with the transformation plan.

Mapping the way forward

When defining a corporate strategy, questions often arise about SAP's role as a software provider. Our customers want to know we have the necessary industry, process, and methodology knowledge and neutrality to provide competent advice.

Business transformation advisors at SAP are exceptionally skilled in answering all those questions using flexible methods and tools developed, based on experiences with various customer projects. For example, advisors leverage SAP Signavio solutions, such as SAP Signavio Process Insights, for the initial process analysis. Then, to evaluate the target architecture, they rely on SAP LeanIX solutions and the SAP Reference Business Architecture tool. This toolbox is based on the TOGAF Standard enterprise architecture methodology with different layers, starting with the business architecture.

It is important to compare the business capabilities derived from the strategy with the SAP solutions and the involvement of required expertise. Such instances will occasionally welcome a special SAP product or industry expert, or even a software developer, who is brought in, in addition, to solve critical challenges. The products that reach the target architecture remain the customer’s decision, supported by SAP’s desire to remain neutral.

Company situations, strategic goals, decision-making processes, and adjustments to the scope, methodology, and service are always possible and naturally move customers away from a standard service. After a more intensive exchange, in my experience, all decision-makers prefer a tailored approach.

Establishing confidence in business changes

At the end of the project, business transformation advisors help ensure the results presented to the management and project team correspond to all necessary decision criteria. Demonstrating the usefulness and necessity of the cooperation between service design and project execution gives involved parties the confidence that the right decisions have been made.

This phase usually comprises the following main deliverables:

  • IT target architecture derived from the business strategy with associated requirements, primarily business capabilities, process scope, IT solutions, and the IT architecture
  • Compatible transformation road map to create a step-by-step and logical sequence while taking into account the dependencies and capacities for realization, such as a suitable deployment model
  • Effort estimate of all resources – including roles, people, and internal and external cost estimations – as well as a proposed cost for software procurement and its operation
  • Cost-benefit analysis, depending on the conditions of the decision-making process (value proposition or business case including ROI, NPV calculation)

Delivering business changes that matter

IT organizations have been trending, for years, toward initiating preliminary efforts as business projects supported by the entire leadership team. Through the involvement and collaboration of corporate management, as well as business and IT representatives in the early phase, this approach helps ensure all interests are considered as agreed and key players are involved and prepared for subsequent projects.

The positive change management aspect of this approach should not be underestimated. Affected employees at all levels should be involved early, especially those from the business. This is a vital success factor in the overall transformation.

The “cloud mindset” and “new way of working” are just two examples that demonstrate the importance of the people-change aspect of transformation. With the increasing footprint of cloud solutions, it is necessary to understand and accept the associated standardization and limitations to individualization. The benefits of combining new processes with modern interfaces are also not to be overlooked – these changes are a prerequisite for younger generations of employees in their daily work.

 

Ready to harmonize your business and IT goals by tailoring your IT strategy with a plan for a solid technology foundation? Read the overview “Your Success Is Our Priority.”

entry.title: Re: Consuming Employee Central OData Services with Java
entry.content: The code and method, is quite outdated, nevertheless it would help if the 3 piece blogs are connected and links are provided.
